prompt: A task has independent security, accessibility, and performance reviews whose findings must be combined into one report. Which pattern is the best fit?

options.text: Run the specialist reviews in parallel, then consolidate their outputs
Have every specialist edit the same file on the same branch simultaneously
Use one agent without any specialization or review
Start consolidation before the specialists produce results

explanation: Independent specialist work is a good fit for fan-out followed by a controlled fan-in or consolidation step. Shared simultaneous edits would create unnecessary conflicts.

source.label: Microsoft Learn — GH-600 multi-agent orchestration objectives

prompt: Two agents discover that they are implementing the same change. What is the best coordination response?

options.text: Record ownership, stop one duplicate task, and preserve any useful findings in a handoff
Let both continue indefinitely on the same branch
Delete all logs so neither agent is favored
Give both agents administrator permissions

explanation: Explicit ownership prevents further duplication. A handoff preserves useful analysis while avoiding competing implementations and unnecessary merge conflicts.

source.label: Microsoft Learn — GH-600 multi-agent conflict objectives

prompt: Which two signals most strongly indicate that an agent execution is stalled? Select two.

options.text: No heartbeat or status update beyond the expected interval
No new artifact, commit, or tool result despite pending work
A detailed final result delivered on time
A completed handoff acknowledged by the next agent

explanation: Stall detection relies on expected liveness and progress signals. Missing heartbeats together with absent observable progress are stronger evidence than output length or a completed handoff.

source.label: Microsoft Learn — GH-600 multi-agent failure objectives

prompt: What is the most useful input for reconstructing why a multi-agent run produced contradictory outputs?

options.text: A correlated timeline of assignments, tool calls, artifacts, decisions, and handoffs
Only the final combined answer
Only the name of the largest model
A list of developer screen resolutions

explanation: Post-hoc analysis needs correlated evidence across agents so reviewers can reconstruct causality, identify stale inputs or conflicting assignments, and locate the point of divergence.

source.label: Microsoft Learn — GH-600 multi-agent observability objectives

prompt: Which two steps reduce disruption when replacing an agent in an active workflow? Select two.

options.text: Stop assigning new work to the old agent and allow or transfer its bounded in-flight tasks
Version the new configuration and preserve a durable handoff of active state
Delete the old agent's artifacts before validation
Change every agent and orchestration rule simultaneously

explanation: A controlled drain or transfer avoids abandoning in-flight work, while versioned configuration and durable state support validation and rollback. Deleting evidence or changing everything at once increases risk.

source.label: Microsoft Learn — GH-600 agent lifecycle objectives

prompt: What is the best way to retire an agent that is no longer needed?

options.text: Disable new assignments, complete or transfer active work, revoke unneeded access, and retain its logs and artifacts
Erase its history immediately
Leave it running with unused credentials
Route new work to it until an error occurs

explanation: Retirement should stop new work, preserve continuity for in-flight tasks, remove unnecessary access, and retain evidence required for audit and post-hoc analysis.

source.label: Microsoft Learn — GH-600 agent lifecycle objectives
